source_metadata.title_raw: Molly S. Stump - A-18-206 - October 9, 2018 - Palo Alto

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 9, 2018


Molly S. Stump
Palo Alto City Attorney
250 Hamilton Avenue, 8th Floor
Palo Alto, CA 94301

Re:	Your Request for Advice
	Our File No. A-18-206

Dear Ms. Stump:

	This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Palo Alto City Councilmember Tom DuBois regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

	Does the Act prohibit Councilmember DuBois from taking part in governmental decisions relating to a proposed Memorandum of Understanding between the City of Palo Alto and the Peninsula Corridor Joint Powers Board (the "Caltrain Board") regarding the electrification of the Caltrain System within the City given that the Councilmember's leased residence is located within 500 feet of the Caltrain tracks and the at-grade crossing at East Meadow Drive?

CONCLUSION

	No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because they would not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his real property interest in his leased residence under Regulation 18702.2(b).


1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-206
Page No. 2

FACTS

The Caltrain Board, a joint powers authority formed pursuant to the Joint Exercise of
Powers Act (Sections 6500 through 6599.3), owns and operates the Caltrain System, which
provides commuter rail service from San Francisco to San Jose. The Caltrain rail corridor bisects
the City along its length, running parallel to Alma Street and passing close by many residential
parcels. Within the City of Palo Alto, there are currently three separated roadway crossings, and
four "at grade" roadway crossings, of the Caltrain tracks.

The Caltrain System currently operates diesel trains, and the Caltrain Board recently began a
project to transition the System from diesel to electric trains. To implement this transition, Caltrain
intends to install new infrastructure supporting electric trains, primarily consisting of aerial wires
("catenary wires") necessary to power the trains. The catenary wires will be installed along
Caltrain's existing right-of-way that encompasses its tracks, and will be supported by columns
installed approximately 150-200 feet apart along the right-of-way. When the infrastructure is in
place, Caltrain will begin adding electric stock to its fleet of trains, eventually replacing most of its
older diesel trains.

While the Caltrain Board owns the right-of-way and possesses certain rights to conduct
work on it, the Caltrain Board desires to enter into a cooperative Memorandum of Understanding
(the "MOU") with municipal jurisdictions along the right-of-way in order to collaboratively address
issues, including the incorporation of local input on design, coordination of work, community
notice, and preservation of trees and vegetation. Caltrain staff and City staff have been discussing
the MOU, and City staff anticipate that the City Council will consider the MOU in the late summer
or early fall of this year.

Councilmember DuBois has a one-year lease on a townhouse located within the City. The
townhouse serves as his primary residence, he moved into the townhouse in April of 2018, and the
townhouse is located within 500 feet of the Caltrain tracks and the at-grade crossing at East
Meadow Drive.

You state that the Councilmember does not intend to continue residing in his leased
townhome; is actively shopping for alternative housing, monitoring real estate listings, and
attending open houses; and intends to purchase a home in the near future. You also state that the
Councilmember's lease may be broken at any point, and he would only be responsible for rent until
the unit is re-rented. You also note that finding a new tenant for a residential rental property
typically does not take long in the City's current rental market.

Caltrain has begun construction of electrification infrastructure in areas outside of the City,
expects to begin construction within the City in one to two years, and anticipates beginning
electrified service in 2022. Caltrain has informed the City that it intends to work primarily at night,
outside of peak-commute hours. Construction impacts, which would be temporary, are likely to
include noise, pollution, and temporary street closures. Because electric trains are expected to be
quieter, faster, and more frequent, "gate down time" at the City's at-grade crossings is anticipated to
occur more often and to last for longer periods of time, leading to increased traffic congestion on
local streets.

File No. A-18-206
Page No. 3

You have asked whether the Act's conflict of interest provisions would prohibit the
Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the MOU because they would have a
disqualifying effect on the Councilmember's real property interest in his leased townhome.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision if that decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the decision's effect on the
public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's financial interests that may give rise to
a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are identified in Section 87103. Of those interests
the facts presented indicate that Councilmember DuBois has a real property interest in his leased
residence, assuming he has an interest in that real property worth $2,000 or more, and an interest in
his personal finances or those of immediate family members, with respect to the decisions at issue.²

Foreseability

Regulation 18701 sets forth the standards for determining whether a decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's interest. Subdivision (a) of that regulation
provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest that is explicitly involved in the decision is
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable; an official's interest is "explicitly involved" in the decision
if it is a "named party in, or the subject of," the decision; and an "interest is the subject of a
proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any
license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest." Subdivision (b) of
that regulation provides the foreseeability standard for a decision's effect on an official's interest
that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, and provides that a decision's effect on an
official's interest that is inexplicitly involved in the decision is reasonably foreseeable if "it can be
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical."

The Councilmember's real property interest in his leased residence is not explicitly involved
in decisions relating to the MOU. Therefore, the effect of those decisions on the Councilmember's
real property interest in his leased residence is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.

Materiality

Regulation 18702.2(b) sets forth the Act's materiality standard applicable to a decision's
effect on an official's real property interest in a leasehold, and provides that a decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on the leasehold is material if the decision will:

(1) Change the termination date of the lease;
(2) Increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property;

² An effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances or those of immediate family members is not
considered separately if the decision also affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property. (Regulation
18702.5(c).) Because the decisions at issue affect the Councilmember's real property interest, we do not further analyze
his personal financial interests or those of his immediate family.

File No. A-18-206
Page No. 4

(3) Increase or decrease the rental value of the property, and the official
has a right to sublease the property;
(4) Change the official's actual or legally allowed use of the real property;
(5) Impact the official's use and enjoyment of the real property.

The real property underlying the Councilmember's leasehold is within 500 feet of the
Caltrain tracks and the at-grade crossing at East Meadow Drive. The decisions relating to the MOU
will not affect the termination date of the Councilmember's lease. Residential real property within
the City is in high demand, and there is no indication from the facts presented that those decisions
will affect the potential rental value of the property. Because the construction of the electrification
infrastructure for the Caltrain tracks within the City will not commence for another one to two
years, the decisions at issue will not affect the present rental value of the real property underlying
the Councilmember's lease. There is no indication from the facts presented that those decisions will
change the Councilmember's actual or legally allowed use of the real property. And finally, the
decisions at issue will not impact the Councilmember's use and enjoyment of the real property
because construction of the electrification infrastructure will not commence until long after the end
of the Councilmember's lease.

Therefore, the decisions relating to the MOU would not have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on the Councilmember's real property interest in his leased residence.
Thus, the Act's conflict of interest provisions do not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part
in those decisions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[illegible]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Councilmember DuBois from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to a proposed Memorandum of Understanding between the City of Palo Alto and the 
Peninsula Corridor Joint Powers Board (the "Caltrain Board") regarding the electrification of the 
Caltrain System within the City given that the Councilmember's leased residence is located within 
500 feet of the Cal train tracks and the at-grade crossing at East Meadow Drive?

because they would not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his real property interest 
in his leased residence under Regulation 18702.2(b ).

QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit Councilmember DuBois from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to a proposed Memorandum of Understanding between the City of Palo Alto and the 
Peninsula Corridor Joint Powers Board (the "Caltrain Board") regarding the electrification of the 
Caltrain System within the City given that the Councilmember's leased residence is located within 
500 feet of the Cal train tracks and the at-grade crossing at East Meadow Drive?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions 
because they would not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his real property interest 
in his leased residence under Regulation 18702.2(b ).
